skill.name: security-audit
skill.description: Review application code for common security vulnerabilities, including SQL injection, shell injection, bare exception handling, path traversal, hardcoded secrets, and XSS.
skill.summary: Guide code reviews with OWASP-style checks, vulnerability examples, and remediation patterns.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, but the reviewed files are markdown security guidance and cookbook examples. I found no executable payload, prompt injection attempt, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent; remaining risk is low because the skill includes command examples that should be run only in authorized environments.

low_findings.title: Static Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The command execution, weak crypto, system reconnaissance, and environment access matches are inside markdown examples, regex scanner patterns, or labeled vulnerable samples. They are used to teach detection and remediation, not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are fenced markdown code blocks and explanatory text with BAD, GOOD, VULNERABLE, or SAFE labels. No executable script file or autonomous command runner was present.

low_findings.title: Security Testing Commands Require Authorization
low_findings.description: The SQL injection cookbook includes security tooling commands such as package installation, linters, sqlmap, and pytest. These are legitimate for security testing but should only be run against systems the user owns or is authorized to test.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in markdown code blocks and are appropriate for this security-audit domain. The sqlmap example targets localhost, which lowers risk but still warrants an authorization warning.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: I searched the scanned files for override language, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, and requests to skip review. No evidence found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search found no suspicious override phrases in the three scanned files. The instruction language is normal workflow guidance for a security review skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples in Markdown
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains examples of os.system, subprocess, grep, package installation, sqlmap, and pytest commands. These are presented as vulnerable examples or authorized audit tooling rather than hidden execution paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The patterns are real command strings, but their semantic context is documentation and security testing guidance. No code path executes them automatically.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential and Environment Variable Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows hardcoded secret examples and environment variable access to explain secure secret handling. These are illustrative values and not operational credential collection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicitly labeled BAD or SAFE and are part of a hardcoded secrets section. I found no network sink or persistence mechanism using these values.

content.value_statement: Security issues often hide in database queries, shell calls, file paths, and exception handling. This skill guides structured reviews with concrete checks and remediation patterns.
actual_capabilities: Identifies high-risk code areas such as authentication, database access, file handling, and user input processing.
Provides review patterns for SQL injection, shell injection, path traversal, hardcoded secrets, XSS, and bare exceptions.
Shows unsafe and safer examples for Python, TypeScript, SQLAlchemy, psycopg2, SQLite, Prisma, and Node-Postgres.
Suggests remediation steps such as parameterized queries, whitelist validation, safer subprocess usage, and specific exception handling.
Produces severity-based audit reports with file, line, pattern, and fix guidance.
limitations: It is guidance for code review, not a full static analysis engine.
It may miss framework-specific security rules unless the project provides them.
Command examples require user approval and authorized test targets.
It does not replace professional penetration testing or compliance review.

use_cases.title: Pre-merge Security Review
use_cases.description: Check changed code for common vulnerabilities before opening or merging a pull request.

use_cases.title: Secure Coding Coaching
use_cases.description: Turn risky patterns into teachable examples with safer alternatives and concise remediation advice.

use_cases.title: Application Risk Triage
use_cases.description: Prioritize SQL, shell, file, secret, and error-handling issues during a focused application review.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Small Change
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a focused diff
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code change for SQL injection, shell injection, hardcoded secrets, path traversal, XSS, and bare exceptions. Report severity and fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Feature Area
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the database, file, and command execution paths in this feature. List confirmed risks, false positives, and safer implementation options.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Remediation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized remediation plan for these security findings. Include impact, likely exploit path, owner action, and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Security Gate
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a lightweight security review gate for this repository. Include patterns to scan, manual review areas, severity rules, and merge criteria.

output_examples.input: Review a user lookup function that builds a query from a request parameter.
output: High severity: possible SQL injection through direct user input in the query.
Use a parameterized query or ORM filter method.
Add tests with common injection payloads and normal input.

output_examples.input: Review a file download endpoint that joins a base directory with a request filename.
output: High severity: possible path traversal if the filename is not resolved and checked.
Resolve the requested path and verify it remains inside the allowed directory.
Reject absolute paths, parent directory segments, and unexpected file types.

output_examples.input: Review an exception handler that catches all failures and returns a default value.
output: Medium severity: broad exception handling can hide defects and operational failures.
Catch specific exceptions and log useful context without sensitive data.
Re-raise unexpected errors so they can be observed and fixed.

best_practices: Review high-risk code paths first, especially user input, database access, files, commands, and authentication logic.
Separate confirmed vulnerabilities from examples, tests, and intentional scanner patterns.
Pair each finding with a concrete fix and a validation step.
anti_patterns: Treating every regex match as a confirmed vulnerability without semantic review.
Running security tools against systems without explicit authorization.
Reporting secrets, tokens, or sensitive data in audit output.

faq.question: Does this skill run a scanner automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance, patterns, and example commands. Users control any tool execution.

faq.question: Which vulnerabilities does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers SQL injection, shell injection, path traversal, hardcoded secrets, XSS, and unsafe exception handling.

faq.question: Can it review Python and TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance includes examples for Python, TypeScript, common database libraries, and general web patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: Yes, when used as review guidance. Run commands only after approval and only against authorized targets.

faq.question: Will it find every vulnerability?
faq.answer: No. It helps with common patterns but does not replace specialist security testing or project-specific rules.

faq.question: How should findings be reported?
faq.answer: Include severity, file location, vulnerable pattern, exploit impact, recommended fix, and verification step.
